name: Cooksey, Frank
resolved_employer: Self-employed attorney/counselor (retired); former Mayor of Austin, 1985-1988
evidence: Frank Cloud Cooksey (1933-2025), retired Austin attorney and 51st Mayor of Austin (1985-1988), a civil-rights trial attorney for DOJ and the Texas AG's office who practiced law ~40 years; a Frank Cooksey in Austin ZIP 78703 listed as 'NOT EMPLOYED' (i.e. retired) made a $100 ActBlue contribution on 09/05/2020, and a LinkedIn profile lists 'Frank Cooksey - Attorney - Self Employed Attorney/Counselor / Retired' in Austin. He was alive and 88 in Oct 2021; the recipient, Ann Howard, is a Democratic Travis County commissioner with an environmental/homelessness portfolio matching Cooksey's lifelong progressive-environmental record. No competing Austin Frank Cooksey with a public political-giving footprint was found. Donor-record ZIP is blank, so identification rests on name rarity plus the corroborating Austin donor record.

affiliations.snippet: Cooksey held the mayoral office from 1985 to 1988; he championed the Comprehensive Watersheds Ordinance protecting Edwards Aquifer water quality and helped establish the Edwards Aquifer Underground Water District.

affiliations.org: U.S. Department of Justice, Civil Rights Division
affiliations.role: Civil rights trial attorney (voting rights cases, Mississippi and Alabama)
affiliations.snippet: Before becoming mayor, Cooksey worked as a civil rights trial attorney for the Department of Justice, specializing in voting rights cases in Mississippi and Alabama.

name: McAfee, Mark
evidence: Public-records aggregators place a Mark T. McAfee, age ~70, at 5003 Ridge Oak Dr, Austin, TX 78731-4727 — an exact match to the donor's ZIP+4 — but no occupation, employer, bio page, or professional profile could be tied to that address. At least 18 Mark McAfees live in Texas, most in the Austin area, with unrelated profiles (GuidePoint Security, Texas Education Agency, SAS data analyst) that cannot be linked to this address. The FEC-lean-1.0 pattern across 102 donations indicates a consistent Democratic small-dollar donor, consistent with the $125 to Brigid Shea, but that alone does not establish an employer. No employer or occupation information exists to support even a categorize-by-employer fallback, so industry is left null.

name: Alter, Alison
resolved_employer: City of Austin (Council Member, District 10, 2017-2025); founder, Alter Advising LLC (philanthropic advising)
evidence: Alison Alter was the sitting Austin City Council Member for District 10 in November 2021, living in the Rosedale neighborhood of Austin; she founded the philanthropic-advising firm Alter Advising in 2013 and holds a Ph.D. from Harvard and a B.A. from Stanford. The name is uncommon in Austin, and a $125 gift from a sitting Democratic council member to Travis County Commissioner Jeff Travillion ahead of his 2022 primary, plus an FEC lean of 1.0 across 57 donations, is consistent with her profile. Confidence is medium rather than high because the donor record carries no ZIP, so identity rests on name rarity and donation pattern rather than an address match. Not a registered Texas lobbyist (the only similar TEC entry is 'Edwards, Alison T.').

affiliations.role: Council Member (2017-2025); Chair, Audit and Finance Committee
affiliations.snippet: Elected to Austin City Council District 10 in December 2016, she serves as Chair of the Audit and Finance Committee and holds memberships on several city committees including Mobility, Austin Energy Oversight, and Austin Water Oversight.
Notable contributions include leading the Ramsey Park renovation campaign, co-founding Just Fund It TX for school finance reform advocacy, and serving on the Parks and Recreation Board.
She launched her own business, a philanthropic advising practice, in 2013; individuals, families and foundations hire Alter Advising to help them pursue their philanthropy more strategically.

name: Ferguson, Frances
resolved_employer: NeighborWorks America (Program Officer/Manager, Austin); Board President, HousingWorks Austin
evidence: Frances Ferguson is an Austin affordable-housing professional: Program Officer at NeighborWorks America, Board President of HousingWorks Austin, founder of Foundation Communities, and formerly a consultant to Catellus on the Mueller redevelopment's affordable-housing plan; she holds an MBA in Real Estate and Finance from UT Austin. An independent contribution record lists Frances Ferguson, NeighborWorks America, Austin 78704, giving $100 to Biden for President on 09/01/2020 — corroborating the heavy Democratic small-dollar pattern (327 donations, lean 1.0). The $125 to Ann Howard, whose portfolio and prior career (ECHO homelessness coalition) sit squarely in Ferguson's affordable-housing/homelessness field, further corroborates identity. Not a registered Texas lobbyist.

affiliations.snippet: Bio page identifies Ferguson as Board President of HousingWorks Austin, an Austin affordable-housing research and policy-advocacy organization.
Ferguson founded Foundation Communities, assembling a board to create a nonprofit with a business model for high-quality rental housing with active resident communities.

Program Officer, NeighborWorks America — a national intermediary providing grants, training, and research/development in community development, focused on nonprofit affordable rental housing providers.

name: House, Melonie
resolved_employer: Retired (former small-business owner); chair/president, Martin Luther King Neighborhood Association and co-chair, East MLK Contact Team; 2022 Austin City Council District 1 candidate
evidence: Melonie House-Dixon of 1602 Astor Place, Austin TX 78721 — an exact zip match to the donor record — is a retired East Austinite who has chaired the MLK Neighborhood Association since 2017 and ran for Austin City Council District 1 in the Nov. 8, 2022 general election, losing to incumbent Natasha Harper-Madison. Her Aug. 24, 2022 gift to Travis County Commissioner Jeff Travillion (Pct. 1, which overlaps her East Austin district) falls during her own council campaign, corroborating the identity. TEC lobbyist registry shows no registration; FEC record shows no federal contributions.

affiliations.snippet: Began community advocacy as Chair for the Martin Luther King Neighborhood Association in 2017 because of the inequities of residing on the East side of I-35.
As Co-Chair of the EMLK Contact Team, she helped negotiate the biggest package of community benefits for a development in East Austin history, at the Regiene Rd project.
She re-established the Bethany Cemetery Association to preserve this historical landmark in District 1, and held community clean ups to honor this sacred space.
Melonie House-Dixon ran for election to the Austin City Council to represent District 1 and lost in the general election on November 8, 2022.

name: Mangan, Andrew

That employer string corresponds to the US BCSD, which Mangan founded and directs. His sustainability/circular-economy career and his advisory seat at UT-Austin's Environmental Science Institute align with a 2017 donation to Brigid Shea, a longtime Austin environmental leader. The 'Suppliers Partnership for the Environment' lead is NOT confirmed: that group's About page names Kellen Mahoney as Executive Director and does not mention Mangan or US BCSD, so that specific attribution is ruled out.

affiliations.snippet: Andy Mangan is co-founder and executive director of the United States Business Council for Sustainable Development (US BCSD), a non-profit association of businesses whose purpose is to create and deliver value-driven sustainable development projects in the United States.
Andrew Mangan is founder and president of Pathway21, a company that enables business-to-business industrial reuse through the award-winning Materials Marketplace software platform.
He currently serves on the board of the Foundation for Sustainable Development.

affiliations.org: Environmental Science Institute, University of Texas at Austin

name: Murphy, Harriet
resolved_employer: City of Austin Municipal Court — judge (retired); previously head of the government department at Huston-Tillotson College and a part-time private practitioner
evidence: Confirmed as Hon. Harriet Mitchell Murphy (1927-2024), the first Black woman appointed to a regular judgeship in Texas (1973), who served 20 years on the Austin Municipal Court including as presiding judge. Identity corroborated by FEC individual-contribution records listing 'Harriet M. Murphy, retired, Austin TX 78754' — an exact zip match to the donor record — giving to the DNC (10/08/2020). She was alive at the time of the 2022-08-24 gift; note the death date is January 17, 2024, not 2023.

affiliations.snippet: In 1973 Murphy became 'the first African-American woman appointed to a regular judgeship in Texas.' She served on the Austin Municipal Court for twenty years.
Inducted into the National Bar Association's Hall of Fame in 2010 'in recognition of her community involvement and of the significant contributions she has made to the cause of justice.'

name: Rutishauser, Robert
resolved_employer: Retired CFO and nonprofit workforce/education executive — founding Executive Director, Capital Area Training Foundation (now Skillpoint Alliance); past president/chair, The Austin Project
evidence: Best match is Robert 'Bob' Rutishauser of Austin (b. 1931, St. Louis; Cornell mechanical engineering; d. 2026), a longtime Austin civic figure: founding Executive Director of the Capital Area Training Foundation/Skillpoint Alliance, president/past chair of The Austin Project, treasurer of Austin Partners in Education, chair of the Austin Community College Foundation board, and 2011 Austin Business Journal legacy CFO of the Year. FEC individual-contribution records show a Robert Rutishauser at Austin zip 78731 making repeated small ActBlue donations ($1-$100, 2018-2019), consistent with the 6,398-donation, 0.988-Democratic-lean small-dollar pattern in this record. Rutishauser is an uncommon surname in Austin, but the donor's zip is unrecorded, so identity is corroborated by donation pattern and city rather than a direct zip match — hence medium, not high. No Texas Ethics Commission lobbyist registration and no ideological/industry PAC giving found; his federal giving appears to be ActBlue/Democratic small-dollar recurring only.

Obituary describes him as the founding Executive Director of the Capital Area Training Foundation, now known as Skillpoint Alliance.
Served as chair of the Austin Community College Foundation Board and treasurer of Austin Partners in Education.

name: Kyba, Ferne
resolved_employer: University of Texas at Arlington, College of Nursing and Health Innovation — Professor Emeritus and former RN-BSN Director (retired 2004)
evidence: Ferne Kyba (b. 1/27/1940) is listed in public records at 2912 Padina Dr, Austin, TX 78733 — an exact match to the donor's zip. She joined the UT Arlington nursing faculty in 1989, taught gerontological nursing, community health nursing and professional/legal/ethical issues, served as RN-BSN Director from 2000 until retiring in 2003-04, and is now a Professor Emeritus and Advisory Council member. She and her late husband Evan Kyba (a UT Austin chemistry professor) endowed the Ferne and Evan Kyba Professorship in Chemistry at UT Austin, and she endowed nursing scholarships at UT Arlington. FEC PAC search and Texas Ethics Commission lobbyist registry search returned no PAC or lobbyist registrations under the Kyba name.
